attributes.stem:
An 84-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with right knee pain that has been present intermittently for several years but has worsened over the past few months. The pain is worse with weight-bearing and improves with rest. He has a medical history of ischaemic heart disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, recurrent urinary tract infections, benign prostatic hyperplasia and previous left knee replacement. His regular medications include metformin, aspirin, perindopril, and atorvastatin.

He is a retired labourer and remains fully independent, living at home with his wife. He is an ex-smoker with a 40 pack-year history and drinks 30 units of alcohol per week.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His right knee demonstrates bony enlargement, crepitus on movement, and reduced range of motion. There is mild joint line tenderness with some effusion and warmth. His gait is antalgic.

Blood tests reveal:

Haemoglobin 138 g/L (130-175)
White cell count 10.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
Platelets 250 x 10^9/L (150-400)
Sodium 139 mmol/L (135-145)
Potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Creatinine 85 µmol/L (60-110)
C-reactive protein 24 mg/L (<5)

X-ray of the right knee shows medial joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, and small subchondral cysts.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of osteoarthritis (OA), including chronic progressive joint pain, bony enlargement, crepitus, and reduced range of motion. Risk factors include advanced age, previous manual labour occupation, and contralateral knee replacement. The relatively normal inflammatory markers and absence of significant warmth or effusion make inflammatory or infectious causes less likely. The X-ray findings are classic for OA.

Osteoarthritis is the most likely diagnosis given the patient's age, chronic progressive symptoms, and examination findings. The previous contralateral knee replacement and history of manual labour suggest a predisposition to OA. The X-ray findings of joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis and cysts are pathognomonic of OA.

Septic arthritis typically presents more acutely with severe pain, marked warmth, swelling, and systemic symptoms such as fever. The normal temperature and only mildly elevated C-reactive protein make this diagnosis unlikely. X-ray changes would show joint space widening due to effusion rather than narrowing.

Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease) can affect the knee but typically presents with acute attacks similar to gout, often with more prominent inflammation than seen here. While chondrocalcinosis may be visible on X-ray, the chronic nature of symptoms and typical OA changes make this less likely.

Gout typically presents with acute episodes of severe joint pain and inflammation, often affecting the first metatarsophalangeal joint initially. While it can affect the knee, the chronic nature of symptoms and minimal inflammation make this less likely. Chronic tophaceous gout can show erosive changes on X-ray.

Rheumatoid arthritis typically presents with symmetric polyarthritis, morning stiffness, and more marked inflammatory markers. The patient's age and pattern of joint involvement make this unlikely. X-ray changes in RA typically show periarticular osteopenia and erosions rather than osteophytes.
learningPoints: Osteoarthritis commonly presents with chronic joint pain that worsens over time, particularly in weight-bearing joints.
Clinical diagnosis of osteoarthritis can often be made based on history and examination findings in patients over 45 years.
Normal inflammatory markers help distinguish osteoarthritis from inflammatory arthritis.
Risk factors for osteoarthritis include age, previous manual labour, obesity, and previous joint injury or surgery.
Classic radiographic features of knee osteoarthritis include joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis and cysts.
references: Allen KD, Thoma LM, Golightly YM. Epidemiology of osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis and cartilage. 2022 Feb 1;30(2):184-95. Available online at ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1063458421008864
Thomas MJ, Guillemin F, Neogi T. Osteoarthritis Flares. Clin Geriatr Med. 2022;38(2):239-257. doi:10.1016/j.cger.2021.11.001. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10523485/

attributes.name: Junior
attributes.description: Suitable for a junior, pre-clinical medical student
